title: Displacement convexity for point processes and an application
speaker: Thomas Leblé
abstract: I will explain how coisotropic submanifolds of symplectic manifolds can be distinguished among all submanifolds by a criterion ("local rigidity") related to the Hofer energy necessary to disjoin open sets from them. This criterion is invariant under symplectic homeomorphisms, leading to a simplified proof of the Humiliere-Leclercq-Seyfaddini theorem that a symplectic-homeomorphic image of a coisotropic submanifold that is smooth is coisotropic.

proof_stages.label: Finite-dimensional displacement-convexity template
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up optimal transport on R^n, displacement interpolation, displacement convexity, and the elementary convexity and quantitative-gain computations for potential and interaction-type functionals that will serve as the model for the later argument.

proof_stages.label: Point processes as infinite-dimensional transport objects
proof_stages.summary: Point configurations and point processes on the line are defined, related heuristically to measures on R^Z via enumeration, and presented as a natural but technically undeveloped setting for optimal transport, forcing an ad hoc construction.

proof_stages.label: Sine-beta variational problem and uniqueness target
proof_stages.summary: The finite-n log gas Gibbs measure and its thermodynamic local limit, the sine-beta point process, lead to an infinite-volume free-energy functional F_beta already known to be minimized by sine beta, and the central theorem is that this minimizer is unique.

proof_stages.label: Energy and entropy densities with convexity heuristic
proof_stages.summary: The energy W and entropy E are defined as limits of finite-box quantities, and the analogy with McCann's interaction and entropy classes suggests displacement convexity because the interaction potential is -log, convex on the positive half-line.

proof_stages.label: Finite-box reduction to classical optimal transport
proof_stages.summary: Since the infinite-volume functionals are defined by finite-box limits, the proof works in large boxes with finitely many points and uses ordinary optimal transport on the corresponding finite-dimensional coordinate space.

proof_stages.label: Volume-order strict displacement gain
proof_stages.summary: Assuming two minimizers, one transports one finite-box restriction to the other and uses convexity of -log to obtain a better interpolated object, with the crucial technical point that the strict gain must be proportional to the box volume so it survives normalization.

proof_stages.label: Pasting to a global competitor and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The improved finite-box processes are pasted by independent copies to create a global competitor, contradicting non-unique minimality and yielding the uniqueness of sine beta as the minimizer, after which the speaker discusses regularity, functional-inequality, and correlation-function questions opened by this characterization.

examples.name: Dirac masses at 0 and 1
examples.note: The pair 0 and 1 makes the difference between ordinary interpolation, which gives a two-atom mixture, and displacement interpolation, which gives the single atom 1/2, concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the Dirac masses at 0 and 1 to compute the usual midpoint as a two-Dirac mixture and the displacement midpoint as the Dirac mass at 1/2 via the translation transport.

examples.name: Bernoulli point process in a box
examples.note: Throwing n uniform points in a finite interval illustrates a point process and how, on the line, a finite configuration can be represented by ordered coordinates in a subset of R^n.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines the Bernoulli point process in a box and concretely identifies it with a uniform measure on a finite-dimensional box by enumerating/sorting the sampled points.

examples.name: Special random-matrix beta values and the sine-kernel limit
examples.note: The audience's reference to familiar beta values such as 1, 2, and 4 and to the local sine-kernel scaling illustrates the random-matrix cases where the sine-beta process is more explicit, in contrast with general beta.
examples.engagement_reason: The special beta values and sine-kernel local limit are only cited as known random-matrix cases or audience familiarity, with no derivation or computation on them.
